content.problemStatement:
Chef has recently joined Exun, a member of the “We not I” legacy, so he wants to apply this motto to a string he received on his birthday!

Chef's string, denoted AAA, is very large.

So, you are instead given a smaller string SSS of length NNN and a positive integer KKK.

Chef's string is then represented by the string A=S+S+S+…+S⏟K timesA = \underbrace{S + S + S + \ldots + S}_{K \text{ times}}A=K timesS+S+S+…+S​​, where +++ denotes string concatenation.

For example, if S="EXUN"S = \text{"EXUN"}S="EXUN" and K=3K = 3K=3, Chef's actual string is "EXUNEXUNEXUN"\text{"EXUNEXUNEXUN"}"EXUNEXUNEXUN".

Note that the length of AAA is exactly N⋅KN\cdot KN⋅K.

As a sign of respect for his club, Chef refuses to have the letter `‘I’` present in the string.

So, Chef will replace every occurrence of `'I'` in his string with any other uppercase English letter of his choice.

Note that different occurrences of `'I'` may be replaced with different characters: for example the string `"IAIA"` can be turned into `"BACA"` or `"EAEA"` or `"JAVA"` (or many other examples.)

Chef defines the score of a string to be the number of adjacent characters that are equal.

Formally, the score of AAA equals the number of indices iii (1≤i<N⋅K1 \le i \lt N\cdot K1≤i<N⋅K) such that Ai=Ai+1A_i = A_{i+1}Ai​=Ai+1​.

Find the maximum possible score Chef's string can attain, after replacing every occurrence of `'I'` with some other letter.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case consists of two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the length of SSS, and the number of times it must be repeated.
The second line will contain the string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum possible score of AAA.
constraints: SSS contains uppercase English letters only, i.e. Si∈{’A’, ’B’,…,’Z’}S_i \in \{\text{'A', 'B',} \ldots, \text{'Z'}\}Si​∈{’A’, ’B’,…,’Z’}.
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5⋅1055\cdot 10^55⋅105

sampleTestCases.input:
6
3 2
IAI
5 1
CILLI
5 3
CILLI
7 2
EXUNITE
5 1000000000
IIIII
20 3
ILOVEEXUNWENOTIIIIII
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We have S="IAI"S = \text{"IAI"}S="IAI" and K=2K = 2K=2. So, Chef's string is A="IAIIAI"A = \text{"IAIIAI"}A="IAIIAI".
It's optimal to turn it into "AAAAAA"\text{"AAAAAA"}"AAAAAA", which has 555 pairs of adjacent equal characters.
Test case 222: We have S="CILLI"S = \text{"CILLI"}S="CILLI" and K=1K = 1K=1. So, Chef's string is A="CILLI"A = \text{"CILLI"}A="CILLI".
One optimal transformation is to make it "CCLLL"\text{"CCLLL"}"CCLLL", which has 333 pairs of adjacent equal characters.
Test case 333: We have S="CILLI"S = \text{"CILLI"}S="CILLI" and K=3K = 3K=3. So, Chef's string is A="CILLICILLICILLI"A = \text{"CILLICILLICILLI"}A="CILLICILLICILLI".
One optimal transformation is to make it "CCLLCCLLLLCCLLL"\text{"CCLLCCLLLLCCLLL"}"CCLLCCLLLLCCLLL", which can be verified to have 999 pairs of adjacent equal characters.
Test case 555: Beware that the answer may exceed the limits of a 32-bit integer.

hidden.input:
6
3 2
IAI
5 1
CILLI
5 3
CILLI
7 2
EXUNITE
5 1000000000
IIIII
20 3
ILOVEEXUNWENOTIIIIII
